141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. 142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:

205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.

\begin{lemma}[Base-ring descent of a localisation along an algebra map]
  \label{lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_restrictScalars_of_algebraMap}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isLocalizedModule_powers_restrictScalars_of_algebraMap}
  Let \(A\) be an \(R\)-algebra, let \(M\) and \(N\) be \(A\)-modules that are also \(R\)-modules
  compatibly with the algebra structure (\(R \to A \to M\) and \(R \to A \to N\) are scalar towers),
  and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be \(A\)-linear. Fix \(f \in R\) and write \(f_A\) for its image
  \(\operatorname{algebraMap}_{R,A}(f) \in A\). If \(\varphi\) exhibits \(N\) as the localisation of
  \(M\) at the powers of \(f_A\) (as \(A\)-modules), then the underlying \(R\)-linear map
  \(\varphi.\operatorname{restrictScalars} R\) exhibits \(N\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the
  powers of \(f\) (as \(R\)-modules).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  This is the converse of Mathlib's \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.of\_restrictScalars}\) (which
  descends a localisation at a submonoid \(S \subseteq R\) to its image in \(A\)); Mathlib does not
  provide this direction, so it is recorded here as a project lemma. It is elementary. The three
  defining clauses of \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) transfer directly. Surjectivity-of-quotients
  and injectivity-up-to-torsion are statements about the underlying additive maps and are unchanged by
  restricting scalars. For the unit clause one must see that multiplication by \(f^k\) on \(N\) is
  bijective as an \(R\)-endomorphism: by the scalar tower \(f^k \cdot x = f_A^{\,k} \cdot x\) for every
  \(x\), so the \(R\)-endomorphism \(\,x \mapsto f^k \cdot x\,\) coincides as a function with the
  \(A\)-endomorphism \(\,x \mapsto f_A^{\,k} \cdot x\,\), and bijectivity is a base-independent property
  of the underlying map (\(\operatorname{Module.End.isUnit\_iff}\)). Hence all three clauses hold over
  \(R\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Image opens of the affine tile identification]
  \label{lem:tile_image_opens_identities}
  % NOTE: the \lean{} pin is deferred until the Lean decl is built; sync will reconcile it.
  \uses{lem:presentation_modulesRestrictBasicOpen}
  Let \(g, f \in R\), let \(R_g = R_g\) be the localisation \(\operatorname{Localization.Away} g\), and
  let \(\iota : \Spec R_g \cong D(g) \hookrightarrow X\) be the open immersion identifying \(\Spec R_g\)
  with the basic open \(D(g) \subseteq X = \Spec R\) (the composite of
  \(\operatorname{basicOpenIsoSpecAway} g\) with the inclusion of \(D(g)\)). Then the image opens of
  \(\iota\) of the two relevant opens of \(\Spec R_g\) are
  \[
    \iota\bigl(\Spec R_g\bigr) = D(g),
    \qquad
    \iota\bigl(D(\bar f)\bigr) = D(gf) = D(g) \cap D(f),
  \]
  where \(\bar f = \operatorname{algebraMap}_{R,R_g}(f)\) is the image of \(f\) in \(R_g\). Equivalently,
  in the notation of the structure-sheaf restriction functors,
  \((\operatorname{specBasicOpen} g).\iota\, ''^{\mathrm U}\,
     \bigl((\operatorname{basicOpenIsoSpecAway} g).\mathrm{inv}\, ''^{\mathrm U}\, \top\bigr)
     = \operatorname{specBasicOpen} g\) and
  \((\operatorname{specBasicOpen} g).\iota\, ''^{\mathrm U}\,
     \bigl((\operatorname{basicOpenIsoSpecAway} g).\mathrm{inv}\, ''^{\mathrm U}\,
       D(\bar f)\bigr) = \operatorname{specBasicOpen}(gf)\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:presentation_modulesRestrictBasicOpen}
  The identification \(\operatorname{basicOpenIsoSpecAway} g\) carries \(\Spec R_g\) isomorphically onto
  \(D(g)\), so the first identity is just the image of the whole space under that isomorphism followed by
  the inclusion. For the second, \(\bar f\) is the image of \(f\) under \(R \to R_g\); its basic open
  \(D(\bar f) \subseteq \Spec R_g\) maps under the identification to \(D(g) \cap D(f)\), since restricting
  to \(D(g)\) and then taking the locus where \(f\) is invertible is the locus where both \(g\) and \(f\)
  are invertible. Finally \(D(g) \cap D(f) = D(gf)\) by \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen\_mul}\)
  (the basic open of a product is the intersection of the basic opens). These are equalities of opens,
  proved set-theoretically; they are not definitional.
\end{proof}
